_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Preble County, OH. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Preble County was formed in 1808 from Montgomery and Butler counties, with Eaton as county seat. The most rural and western county in the cluster, Preble County has strong German Baptist Brethren (Dunker) and Quaker heritage and holds continuous records from formation.

previous_courthouses.location: Eaton, brick courthouse on Main Street
Eaton, larger courthouse on same site
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the 1918 building. Records survived transfer.

record_losses.description: Preble County has experienced no courthouse fire and no significant record loss. The county's inland location, low flood risk, and rural character contributed to record preservation. Records run continuously from the 1808 formation. For ancestors present before 1808, researchers should check Montgomery and Butler county records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Preble County's intact records mean that gaps in {ancestor_name}'s paper trail reflect individual circumstances — tenancy, illiteracy, or the informal nature of rural transactions — rather than courthouse disasters. For the period before 1808, the parent county records in Montgomery (Dayton) and Butler (Hamilton) should be checked.
record_losses.source: History of Preble County, Ohio. Cleveland: H. Z. Williams & Bro., 1881.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Pre-1808 transactions in this territory appear in Montgomery and Butler county deed books.
deeds.repository: Preble County Recorder, Eaton; Ohio History Connection microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com OH land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — probate records from county formation. Estate inventories are valuable for documenting the material lives of farming families.
wills_estates.gaps: Early loose papers fragile but generally legible.
wills_estates.repository: Preble County Probate Court, Eaton; Ohio History Connection

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records from county formation
marriages.gaps: German Baptist Brethren marriages were often performed within the congregation before obtaining civil licenses. Quaker marriages were recorded in meeting minutes. Both groups eventually complied with civil requirements, but early records may show gaps where religious ceremonies preceded civil registration.
marriages.repository: Preble County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — Court of Common Pleas minutes from formation
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace records incomplete.
court_minutes.repository: Preble County Clerk of Courts; Ohio History Connection

vital_records.completeness: Ohio statewide registration began 1908. Rural compliance was slow — Preble County's farming communities were less diligent about registration than urban areas. Some probate court records of births/deaths from the 1860s-1907.
vital_records.repository: Preble County Probate Court; Ohio Department of Health; Preble County District Library

substitute_sources.description: The Church of the Brethren had one of its strongest concentrations in the Miami Valley in Preble and adjacent counties. Congregations maintained membership rolls, minutes, baptism records, and correspondence. The Brethren tradition of communal discipline and mutual accountability produced records that document community life in unusual detail.
substitute_sources.repository: Brethren Historical Library and Archives, Elgin, IL; individual congregations; Preble County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Brethren church records document families who migrated as religious communities from Pennsylvania and Virginia. Membership transfers between congregations track migration along the Brethren network. Disciplinary records document family disputes and social tensions. The Brethren emphasis on community meant that church records effectively serve as community censuses.
substitute_sources.source: Durnbaugh, Donald F. Fruit of the Vine: A History of the Brethren, 1708-1995. Elgin, IL: Brethren Press, 1997.

substitute_sources.source_type: Quaker meeting records — West Branch and other meetings
substitute_sources.description: Quaker families from Virginia and the Carolinas settled in Preble County, establishing monthly meetings that maintained detailed records of births, marriages, deaths, and membership transfers.
substitute_sources.repository: Wilmington College Peace Resource Center; Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College; Ohio History Connection
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Certificates of removal document migration chains from Carolina and Virginia meetings. Quaker records name both parents in birth entries and list witnesses at marriages. The Quaker migration to free-soil Ohio was often motivated by opposition to slavery, and meeting minutes sometimes document the moral reasoning behind relocation.
substitute_sources.source: Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy. Vol. 4, Ohio.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1810–1940. Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) are the primary source for documenting Preble County's farming economy.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Agricultural schedules are critical for Preble County — they document farm size, crops, livestock, and farm value for every farm operator. In a county where nearly everyone farmed, these schedules are as important as the population schedules.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Preble County, OH.

narrative_hooks: Preble County's records run from 1808 without a break — no fire, no flood, no loss. If {ancestor_name} is not in the record, it is because {pronoun_subject} did not leave a mark that the county clerk recorded, not because the record was destroyed.

If {ancestor_name} was German Baptist Brethren, the church records at the Elgin archives may document {pronoun_possessive} family more thoroughly than the courthouse — membership rolls, disciplinary proceedings, and letters of transfer that tracked families from Pennsylvania to Ohio.

The Quaker certificate of removal is the migration document that the courthouse never created — it tracks {ancestor_name}'s family from a Virginia or Carolina meeting to the Ohio frontier, recording the community's approval of their departure and the receiving meeting's acceptance.
Before 1808, Preble County did not exist — {ancestor_name}'s earliest records are in Montgomery or Butler county, and researchers who stop at the Preble County line miss the first chapter.
source_references: History of Preble County, Ohio. Cleveland: H. Z. Williams & Bro., 1881.
Durnbaugh, Donald F. Fruit of the Vine: A History of the Brethren, 1708-1995. Elgin, IL: Brethren Press, 1997.
Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy. Vol. 4, Ohio. Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1946.
Ohio History Connection. County Records Guide: Preble County.
